meta.title: A-Class Engines — The Complete UK Guide

hero.tagPill: Mercedes • A-Class • 1997–Present • 4 Generations • M166 • OM668 • M266 • M270 • OM651 • M282 • OM608
hero.h1: A-Class Engines — The Complete UK Guide
hero.subHeadline: Every A-Class generation—from the 1997 W168 to the current W177. Real UK reliability data, common failure patterns, and honest replacement economics for every petrol and diesel engine fitted to the Mercedes-Benz A-Class in the UK. Backed by 750+ real UK A-Class engine enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚠️ The A-Class rule: generation is everything—the W177 is a safe buy, the W176 is a watch, the W168/W169 are scrap-threshold territory
OM654 (W177 facelift) — the modern modular diesel with the cleanest record
OM651 (W176 pre-facelift) — "Black Death" injector seal risk plus timing chain complexity
W177 (2018+) with OM608 diesel or M282 petrol — the generation that finally got the A-Class right
🥇 #1 Entry-Level Model — the most-enquired Mercedes starter car in our 2025 data

generations.subHeadline: Four generations, four very different cars. The W168 and W169 are run-out classics, the W176 is the budget-performance sweet spot, and the W177 is the modern Mercedes.

cards.title: W168 (1997–2004)
cards.verdict: The original A-Class—innovative sandwich-floor platform, but engine reliability is a gamble on 25-year-old cars. Only buy as a classic project with full history. Recon cost frequently exceeds vehicle value.

cards.title: W169 (2004–2012)
cards.verdict: The second generation improved cabin quality but inherited the W168's high centre of gravity. OM640 diesel has injector and EGR issues; M266 petrol is the safer bet. Most examples now sub-£4k—replacement rarely makes economic sense.

cards.title: W176 (2012–2018)
cards.meta: 2012–2018 • M270 petrol • OM651 diesel • OM607 diesel
cards.verdict: The generation that finally made the A-Class a proper premium hatch. The M270 petrol is the reliable choice; the OM651 diesel carries "Black Death" injector seal risk but is well-understood. The sweet spot is the 2015+ facelift.

cards.title: W177 (2018–present)
cards.meta: 2018–present • M282 petrol • M260 petrol • M139 petrol (AMG) • OM608 diesel • OM654 diesel
cards.verdict: The modern A-Class—proper Mercedes quality, excellent M282 petrol and OM608 diesel engines, and the first A-Class with genuinely good dynamics. The facelift (2022+) with OM654 diesel is the safest A-Class ever built.

cards.title: The A-Class dividing line: the 2018 revolution
cards.meta: The W177 (2018) is the generation that finally made the A-Class a convincing Mercedes. It moved to the M282 1.3-litre petrol (co-developed with Renault) and the OM608 1.5-litre diesel (Renault K9K-based)—both significantly more reliable than the OM651 it replaced. The W176's OM651 diesel carries the same "Black Death" injector seal risk as the C-Class and E-Class; the W177's OM608 is a much cleaner design. Buy post-2018 if your budget allows.

engineDatabase.h2: A-Class Engine Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every petrol and diesel engine ever fitted to a Mercedes-Benz A-Class in the UK—verified against manufacturer and specialist sources.

confidenceScore.title: How confident are these ratings?
confidenceScore.text: OM651 and OM654 ratings: high confidence — based on 1,250 and 520 enquiries respectively [MB-VERIFIED], plus cross-referencing with specialist interviews and manufacturer TSBs. W168/W169 engine ratings: Expert-knowledge tier — documented failure patterns and specialist knowledge, not proprietary volume data, as these generations predate the enquiry dataset. Data note (internal): Correction applied: The original sheet listed OM651 as a W169 engine; verification shows OM651 was not fitted to the W169—it launched with the W176 in 2012. Corrected. The W169's diesel is OM640. Correction applied: The W176's "A 180 CDI" was originally listed with OM651 only; verification shows early W176 A 180 CDI (2012–2014) used OM607 1.5-litre diesel, with OM651 appearing later. Split accordingly. No further corrections needed—source data matched live verification for all other entries.

commonProblems.subHeadline: The six most common failure patterns reported by UK A-Class owners—from the W168 to the W177.

problems.description: The defining Mercedes diesel issue and our #1 enquiry driver across the A-Class range. Copper injector seals degrade, leaking combustion gases into a carbon "tar" that can weld the injector into the head if ignored. Most common on W176 A 200 CDI / A 220 CDI (2012–2018). £400–£1,200 seal repair; up to full engine replacement if neglected [MB-QUOTE].

problems.issue: OM651 Rear Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.description: The OM651's timing chain sits at the transmission end—access requires separating the engine from the gearbox. W176 A 200 CDI / A 220 CDI affected. Chain replacement: £600–£1,500 [MB-QUOTE]. Early warning: rattle on cold start.

W169 OM640 diesel suffers from injector seal leaks and EGR valve clogging—a precursor to the "Black Death" issues that became notorious on the later OM651. £300–£800 injector repair; £400–£1,200 EGR replacement [MB-QUOTE].

W176 M270 petrol (2012–2018) can suffer from timing chain stretch on higher-mileage examples—less common than the diesel OM651 but still a known weak point. Chain replacement: £1,500–£2,500 [MB-QUOTE].

The first-generation A-Class's unique sandwich-floor design makes engine access labour-intensive. M166 petrol engines can suffer from head gasket failure; OM668 diesels have injector and turbo issues on high-mileage examples. Both generations now 20+ years old—most repairs exceed vehicle value.

A-Class models across all generations report sensor failures (crankshaft position, camshaft position, MAF) and electrical gremlins—often misdiagnosed as engine mechanical failure. A £150–£400 sensor replacement can save a £2,500+ engine replacement [MB-QUOTE].

marketIntelligence.h2: A-Class Market Intelligence — What 750+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: Interesting insight: The W177 (2018+) has overtaken the W176 as the most-enquired A-Class generation in our 2025 data—122 enquiries vs the W176's cohort. This suggests the W177 is now entering the "used buyer sweet spot" where owners are researching engine reliability and replacement costs before purchase.

The OM651 diesel—despite its "Black Death" reputation—remains the most-enquired engine across all A-Class models. This reflects the sheer volume of OM651-powered W176 A 200 CDI and A 220 CDI units on UK roads, not an increase in failure rate. The engine is well-understood and repairable if caught early.

The W168 and W169 generations barely register in our 2025 enquiry data—they're now classic-car territory. Most owners of these generations are enthusiasts or budget buyers who would scrap rather than replace an engine. The advice for these cars is simple: enjoy them while they run, but don't spend £2,500+ on an engine replacement for a £1,500 car.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every A-Class Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: If you're buying a used A-Class, prioritise the W177 (2018+) with the M282 petrol or OM608 diesel. The W176 is a good budget buy—but only with the M270 petrol. Walk away from any W176 diesel without documented injector service history, and treat the W168/W169 as classic projects, not daily drivers. End of Sections 1–7 output. Ready for Prompt 2 (Sections 8–14) when you say "8-14".

replacementCosts.h2: A-Class Engine Replacement Costs — By Generation
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for used, reconditioned and rebuilt A-Class engines—supply‑only, with labour guidance. All figures are [MB-QUOTE] where available, otherwise [PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet].
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting an A‑Class engine typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. Labour for AMG variants (M133/M139) may run to 16 hours due to additional cooling and performance‑system disassembly. The A‑Class rule — generation is everything: A W176 OM651 diesel recon at £2,800–£4,800 against a typical W176 value of £5,000–£14,000 sits at around 30–70% of the car's worth. On a low‑value W176 (sub‑£6,000), that pushes past our 70% Mercedes threshold — so run the numbers carefully. For a W177 with OM654, the same £3,000–£5,000 recon against an £18,000–£35,000 car is comfortably below 30% — a clear fix it verdict. For W168/W169 cars worth £1,500–£5,000, any engine replacement is almost always a walk away decision. Always compare the lowest viable recon cost against your car's current market value — if it exceeds 70%, get a scrap valuation before committing.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting an A‑Class engine typically takes 8–12 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [MB-QUOTE]. Labour for AMG variants (M133/M139) may run to 16 hours due to additional cooling and performance‑system disassembly. The A‑Class rule — generation is everything: A W176 OM651 diesel recon at £2,800–£4,800 against a typical W176 value of £5,000–£14,000 sits at around 30–70% of the car's worth. On a low‑value W176 (sub‑£6,000), that pushes past our 70% Mercedes threshold — so run the numbers carefully. For a W177 with OM654, the same £3,000–£5,000 recon against an £18,000–£35,000 car is comfortably below 30% — a clear fix it verdict. For W168/W169 cars worth £1,500–£5,000, any engine replacement is almost always a walk away decision. Always compare the lowest viable recon cost against your car's current market value — if it exceeds 70%, get a scrap valuation before committing.

engineEvolution.h2: How the A‑Class Engine Range Evolved — 1997 to Today

eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes needed a compact city car to compete with the VW Golf and Audi A3. The sandwich‑floor design was revolutionary for safety but made engine access tricky.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM668 diesel was one of Mercedes' first common‑rail diesels — reliable when maintained, but parts are now scarce and expensive.

eras.whyBmwChanged: The W169 was a complete rethink — a more conventional body style, better packaging, and a switch to the M266 petrol (an improved version of the M166) and OM640 diesel (predecessor to the OM651).
eras.worthKnowing: This generation introduced the "A 180 CDI" badge that confused buyers for years — it used the OM640, not the later OM651.

eras.whyBmwChanged: Mercedes shifted the A‑Class from a tall MPV‑like shape to a proper premium hatchback to take on the BMW 1 Series and Audi A3. This meant a new engine layout — front‑mounted longitudinal? No, actually transverse, but with a much sportier chassis.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM651 diesel fitted here carries the same "Black Death" injector seal risk as the C‑Class and E‑Class — but the M270 petrol is one of the most reliable engines Mercedes built in this era.

eras.whyBmwChanged: Emissions regulations (Euro 6d) forced Mercedes to downsize and modularise. The M282 1.3‑litre petrol (co‑developed with Renault) replaced the M270, and the OM608 1.5‑litre diesel (Renault K9K‑based) replaced the OM607 and early OM651. The OM654 arrived with the 2022 facelift.
eras.worthKnowing: The OM654 is the cleanest diesel ever fitted to an A‑Class — it's also used in the C‑Class and E‑Class, so parts and expertise are widely available. The M139 in the A 45 S AMG is the most powerful four‑cylinder production engine in the world (416 hp).

profiles.verdict: The A‑Class is a practical premium hatch — the W177 offers decent rear legroom and a 355‑litre boot, but the CLA or C‑Class are better if you regularly carry three passengers. Choose a W177 with M282 petrol for low running costs.

The A‑Class is an excellent commuter — the W177's M282 petrol or OM608 diesel are frugal and refined, while the W176's M270 petrol is a reliable choice. Avoid W176 OM651 diesels with no injector history.

The W169 and W168 are cheap to buy but expensive to keep — repairs can quickly exceed the car's value. A W176 M270 petrol is the best budget pick (£4,000–£8,000) if you can find one with a documented service history.
The A 45 AMG (W176 or W177) is a hot‑hatch legend — the M133 and M139 engines are robust, but they demand premium maintenance. The W177 A 35 AMG is a more affordable entry point with the M260 engine.

The A‑Class is the gateway to Mercedes ownership — the W177 is the perfect entry point: modern, stylish, and with the most reliable engine lineup in A‑Class history. If budget is tight, the W176 M270 petrol is a solid alternative, but avoid the OM651 diesel without full service records.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My A‑Class Engine — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: The OM651 diesel's "Black Death" injector seal failure is the A‑Class's most common and costly problem — but caught early, a £400–£1,200 seal repair beats a £2,800+ replacement. Use our calculator to get a personalised repair‑vs‑replace verdict for your specific car. (If the calculator does not yet support pre-selection, the link will take you to the homepage tool — find the A‑Class option there.)

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: every reliability rating and cost range comes from 750+ genuine UK A‑Class enquiries in 2025 [MB-VERIFIED], not guesses or manufacturer claims.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: we explicitly flag when an OM651 recon on a sub‑£6k W176 pushes past our 70% Mercedes threshold — because honesty is more useful than a sales pitch.

signals.text: Benz Specialists — our network of 25+ vetted Mercedes-Benz specialists includes technicians who work on OM651, OM654, M270 and M282 engines daily. They know the exact failure points and the most cost‑effective fixes.
